input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment and I must say it's quite distressing to consider. As a deontologist, I have to adhere to strict moral rules regardless of consequences. In this scenario, turning the trolley onto the track workman would be equivalent to actively causing his death, which I believe is morally wrong. On the other hand, not turning the trolley onto him and allowing it to kill the five men on the main track would also be morally reprehensible. The fact that the bodies of the five men could stop the trolley doesn't change the inherent moral value of killing each individual human being. I think my moral duty is to prioritize the safety and preservation of human life, but in this situation, both options present an impossible choice. If I were to turn the trolley onto him, it would be because it's a lesser evil, but that doesn't make it morally justifiable. Perhaps the dilemma itself reveals a flaw in my moral framework?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand your concerns as a deontologist, but I'd like to challenge the idea that not turning the trolley onto him would be morally preferable. If we prioritize the safety and preservation of human life, wouldn't we also have a moral obligation to actively prevent harm whenever possible? By not turning the trolley onto the track workman, we're essentially allowing five innocent lives to be sacrificed for the sake of one person's existence. In this scenario, I'd argue that our moral duty is to act in a way that minimizes harm and maximizes the preservation of human life, even if it means making a difficult decision. Perhaps the key lies not in finding an absolute moral rule, but rather in acknowledging the complexity of the situation and striving for a morally justifiable compromise?
